summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s shift from tools to people and process misses a deeper constraint: most companies don’t have data with the leverage of Silicon Valley giants. The modern data stack looks powerful, but for many businesses the data is more like peat than oil—limited value means copying big-tech practices often yields disappointing returns.
money_quotes: Tools are out; people and processes are in.
In other words, rather than being exemplars of good data practices because they were so good, it’s possible that they’re exemplars because they didn’t have to be that good.
The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of an Amazon’s browsing logs.

Peat suppliers can’t copy the refinement methods and cost structures of oil producers and still expect to come out ahead; small SaaS businesses can’t operate as though their data has anywhere near the same value as Uber’s or Airbnb’s.
If there’s a reckoning in the data industry, it’ll come from our expectations of data coming back to earth.
key_insight: Most companies overestimate the value of their data, so emulating big-tech data practices and tooling often leads to waste rather than outsized gains.
